title: Bootstrap Anchor, frontend, and automation workspace
description:
Create the minimum runnable workspace needed to implement Meridian across program, frontend, and automation packages.

Scope:
- Establish the Anchor workspace and package layout.
- Add frontend and automation package skeletons with shared config boundaries.
- Define environment variable surfaces and add .env.example.
- Add reproducible local commands for build, test, and devnet deployment.
- Keep the setup minimal and implementation-oriented.

Done when:
- A new developer can install dependencies and run the core local commands.
- Program, frontend, and automation code have a stable home in the repo.
- The repo is ready for implementation work without structural churn.

title: Add merge primitive and define Sell No close path
description:
Make merge a first-class protocol primitive so Sell No has a clean pre-settlement close path.

Scope:
- Add merge to the required contract surface.
- Define merge semantics: burn equal Yes and No, return equal USDC from the vault, pre-settlement only.
- Specify account checks, authority rules, and invariant preservation.
- Define the baseline Sell No flow as buy Yes then merge.
- Treat one-click atomic Sell No as a later Phoenix composition task, not a prerequisite for protocol correctness.

Done when:
- Merge is part of the implementation plan and test plan.
- Sell No is no longer a protocol gap.
- The contract and frontend can target a correct two-step flow before atomic UX work.
close_reason: Documented lifecycle and merge decisions in requirements and research docs

title: Core feature epic: daily automation and market operations
description:
Deliver the off-chain operating layer that creates markets each morning and settles them each afternoon.

Scope:
- Generate strikes from the previous close and handle deduplication.
- Create daily markets idempotently across supported tickers.
- Run the settlement job with retries and alerting hooks.
- Handle failure paths and manual override escalation.
- Manage operational configuration for schedules, feed mappings, and thresholds.

Done when:
- Daily market creation and settlement can run reproducibly on devnet.
- Operational failure paths are explicit and recoverable.
- The system no longer depends on manual step-by-step operator intervention for the normal path.

title: Meridian MVP roadmap: core feature epics
description:
Goal: define the high-level implementation plan for Meridian as a set of core feature epics.

This roadmap starts after the foundation work in br-guy and organizes implementation around product capabilities rather than setup tasks.

Execution order:
1. On-chain market engine
2. Phoenix trading flows
3. Oracle settlement and redemption
4. Daily automation and market operations
5. Trader-facing frontend experience
6. Devnet deployment, testing, and demo readiness

Done when:
- Each core feature has a dedicated epic with a clear product boundary.
- The roadmap is implementation-oriented and sequenced for delivery.
- Follow-on tasks can be attached under the relevant feature epic instead of accumulating under one generic plan.

title: Freeze market lifecycle and settlement semantics
description:
Define the simplest explicit market lifecycle for Meridian and remove ambiguity from the requirements.

Scope:
- Formalize 4:00 PM ET market close behavior.
- Decide whether minting, new orders, and cancellations are allowed after 4:00 PM ET.
- Define how Phoenix resting orders expire and whether market status is switched explicitly.
- Lock the settlement timing rule, retry window, and admin override delay.
- Produce one canonical lifecycle/state-transition table for create -> active -> closed -> settled -> redeemable.

Done when:
- The lifecycle is written down in implementation-ready terms.
- There is no remaining ambiguity about what the protocol and automation service do at and after 4:00 PM ET.
- Follow-on contract and automation work can treat these rules as fixed.
close_reason: Documented lifecycle and merge decisions in requirements and research docs

title: Define strike generation, automation boundaries, and failure paths
description:
Turn the daily lifecycle requirements into deterministic automation behavior.

Scope:
- Define strike-generation rules and deduplication behavior.
- Define idempotent morning market creation behavior.
- Define settlement job behavior, retries, and manual override handoff.
- Define config surfaces for market hours, ticker mapping, and operational thresholds.
- Clarify what the automation service owns versus what the program enforces.

Done when:
- Morning and settlement jobs have stable boundaries.
- Failure paths are explicit rather than implicit.
- Automation work can start without reopening product-level questions.

title: Core feature epic: oracle settlement and redemption
description:
Deliver the close-of-day resolution path that turns live markets into settled outcomes and pays winners correctly.

Scope:
- Read the settlement price from Pyth using the agreed regular-session rule.
- Validate staleness and confidence thresholds.
- Resolve each market outcome immutably.
- Support admin override settlement under the defined delay and emergency rules.
- Support post-settlement redemption of winning tokens.

Done when:
- Markets can be settled correctly on devnet.
- Winning token holders can redeem USDC end to end.
- Settlement logic is testable across above, below, and at-strike cases.

title: Core feature epic: Phoenix trading and four trade flows
description:
Deliver the trading layer on top of Meridian markets using Phoenix as the CLOB.

Scope:
- Create and manage Yes/USDC Phoenix markets for each strike.
- Support Buy Yes and Sell Yes directly on the book.
- Support Buy No via mint-and-sell-Yes composition.
- Support Sell No via buy-Yes then merge, with atomic composition as a later enhancement.
- Expose order-book views that support both Yes and No perspectives.

Done when:
- All four trade paths are supported by correct protocol and integration flows.
- Phoenix market creation and order placement are stable on devnet.
- Trading behavior can feed the frontend without architectural rework.

title: Core feature epic: on-chain market engine
description:
Build the on-chain Meridian program capabilities that define a market and manage collateral before trading and settlement concerns.

Scope:
- Initialize global config and authority surfaces.
- Create strike markets with Yes/No mints and collateral vaults.
- Support mint pair and merge pair flows.
- Define pause controls and core state transitions.
- Enforce the non-negotiable collateral and token invariants on-chain.

Done when:
- A market can be created deterministically on devnet.
- Users can mint and merge pairs correctly.
- The program state model is stable enough for trading and settlement integration.

title: Core feature epic: trader-facing frontend experience
description:
Deliver the user-facing application for discovery, trading, portfolio management, and redemption.

Scope:
- Landing and markets views with wallet connection and live market discovery.
- Trade view with Yes/No perspectives over one Phoenix order book.
- Position-aware trade controls for Buy Yes, Buy No, Sell Yes, and Sell No.
- Portfolio and history views with positions, outcomes, and redeem actions.
- Clear presentation of countdowns, prices, and payout semantics.

Done when:
- A user can navigate from discovery to trade to portfolio to redemption in one coherent app flow.
- The frontend correctly abstracts the one-book / two-perspective trading model.
- Wallet signing and transaction status flows are stable enough for demo use.

title: Spike Phoenix integration for market creation and trading flows
description:
Reduce Phoenix integration risk before committing to full implementation.

Scope:
- Validate custom Yes/USDC market creation on devnet.
- Validate seat approval and market authority workflow.
- Prove the basic order-placement path we need for Meridian.
- Identify the exact path for later atomic Buy No and Sell No composition.
- Capture any Phoenix-specific constraints that affect account design or UX.

Done when:
- Phoenix risks are reduced to concrete implementation tasks.
- We know the exact integration boundaries between the Anchor program, automation, and frontend.
- There are no major unknowns left about market creation or seat handling.

title: Core feature epic: devnet deployment, testing, and demo readiness
description:
Deliver the proof that Meridian works end to end on devnet and can be reproduced by another developer or reviewer.

Scope:
- Reproducible setup, build, and deployment commands.
- Program, integration, and frontend test coverage for the MVP path.
- End-to-end devnet flow for create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.
- Demo-oriented scripts and environment configuration.
- Clear README-level operating instructions for running the system locally and on devnet.

Done when:
- Another developer can stand up the system and reproduce the core lifecycle.
- The project has a realistic quality bar for shipping the MVP demo.
- Demo readiness is no longer implicit or manual.

title: Spike Pyth settlement and feed handling on Solana devnet
description:
Reduce Pyth integration risk for settlement and daily market operations.

Scope:
- Verify the devnet feed configuration for all supported tickers.
- Prove the pull-oracle update flow needed for settlement.
- Define staleness and confidence checks in implementation-ready terms.
- Confirm the simplest settlement read rule for the regular session.
- Capture any SDK or Anchor compatibility constraints that affect implementation.

Done when:
- The settlement oracle path is implementation-ready.
- Feed configuration and validation rules are documented.
- Remaining Pyth work is reduced to straightforward coding tasks.

title: Define core account model and on-chain invariants
description:
Translate the requirements into a concrete Anchor program state model before feature implementation.

Scope:
- Define config, market, vault, mint, and authority accounts.
- Define PDA strategy and seed conventions.
- Define settlement states and allowed transitions.
- Specify mint, merge, settle, redeem, and pause invariants in implementation terms.
- Clarify what must be enforced on-chain versus only in the frontend.

Done when:
- The program account graph is stable enough to implement against.
- The core invariants are written in a form that can drive tests.
- There is no ambiguity about the contract boundaries for the first implementation pass.

title: Meridian foundation: architecture freeze and build scaffolding
description:
Goal: establish the technical and product foundation required before full feature implementation for Meridian.

First steps:
1. Freeze the core architecture and assumptions from the requirements: Solana devnet, Anchor, Phoenix, Pyth, settlement source-of-truth, 4:00 PM ET lifecycle behavior, merge requirement for Sell No, strike rounding/deduplication, admin authority model, and MVP demo scope.
2. Bootstrap the workspace and developer tooling: Anchor program workspace, TypeScript packages for frontend and automation, shared config, .env.example, reproducible dev/test commands, and deployment script skeletons.
3. Define the protocol state model and invariants in implementation terms: config account, strike market account, vault, Yes/No mints, settlement state machine, merge flow, redemption flow, and explicit on-chain invariant checks.
4. Prove critical external integrations with focused spikes: Phoenix market creation and seat workflow, Phoenix CPI for atomic Buy No and Sell No close path, Pyth pull-update settlement read, and devnet USDC handling.
5. Establish deterministic market operations: strike-generation module, idempotent daily market creation plan, automation job boundaries, and failure/override paths.
6. Create the initial testing baseline: invariant tests, settlement edge cases, and one happy-path end-to-end flow target for create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.

Done when:
- Architecture decisions and assumptions are documented and internally consistent with the requirements.
- The repo has a runnable scaffold for program, frontend, and automation work.
- The protocol state model is clear enough to begin implementation without re-litigating core flows.
- Phoenix and Pyth feasibility risks have been reduced to concrete implementation tasks.
- Follow-on child issues can be created directly from this epic without further discovery.

title: Establish initial invariant and end-to-end test plan
description:
Define the first testing slice needed to build safely from the start.

Scope:
- Define invariant tests for vault collateralization and payout conservation.
- Define settlement edge cases: above strike, below strike, exactly at strike, stale oracle, and wide confidence.
- Define the first happy-path integration target: create -> mint -> trade -> settle -> redeem.
- Identify which parts run locally versus on devnet.
- Keep the first pass narrow enough to support rapid implementation.

Done when:
- The first implementation tasks know what tests they are expected to satisfy.
- The project has a clear minimum quality bar before expanding scope.
- Full-lifecycle testing is broken into achievable milestones.
